Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee

June 18-19, 2025

Developments in Financial Markets and Open Market Operations

Financial markets showed mixed performance during the intermeeting period. Equity prices declined amid concerns about inflation persistence. Treasury yields increased across the curve, reflecting expectations of continued monetary policy tightening.

Market participants highlighted uncertainties regarding the path of future rate decisions and their impact on economic growth. Options-implied volatility remained elevated compared to historical averages.

Developments in the Economy and Economic Outlook

Recent data indicated that economic activity continued to expand at a moderate pace. Consumer spending showed resilience, supported by solid labor market conditions. Business investment remained subdued amid higher borrowing costs.

Housing sector activity softened further, reflecting elevated mortgage rates. The outlook for economic growth over the medium term was revised slightly downward, reflecting the cumulative effects of monetary policy tightening.

Staff Economic Outlook

The staff forecast for real GDP growth was revised down modestly for the current year, reflecting weaker-than-expected household spending. The unemployment rate projection was adjusted upward slightly. Inflation was expected to remain above target in the near term before gradually converging to 2 percent.

Discussion of Economic Conditions and Policy

Participants discussed recent economic developments and their implications for monetary policy. Many participants noted that inflation remained elevated and that further evidence of disinflation would be needed to inform future policy decisions.

Several participants emphasized the importance of data dependence and the need to assess incoming information comprehensively. The Committee agreed that risks to the economic outlook remained two-sided.

Staff Review of the Financial Situation

Financial conditions tightened modestly during the intermeeting period. Banks reported continued strong loan demand, particularly from commercial and industrial sectors. Credit spreads widened somewhat but remained within historical ranges.

Committee Policy Action

The Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent. The decision reflected continued elevated inflation pressures and the strength of the labor market.

Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S. Barr. Voting against the action was Thomas I. Barkin.
